description:
A monitoring specialist wants to prove that her daily log-directory backup
  procedure works before wiring it into the company’s alerting system.

  Your goal is to perform ONE manual “dry-run” for the date
  2025-01-01 and leave behind the artefacts that the specialist’s
  automated tests will later look for.

  1. Source data  
     • The directory /home/user/app/logs already exists and contains a single
       text file called app.log (leave this file untouched; you only need to
       archive it).

  2. Required artefacts (must all be created inside the user’s $HOME):  
     a) A gzip-compressed tar archive named  
        /home/user/backups/logs_backup_2025-01-01.tar.gz  
        which, when extracted, produces a top-level directory called
        logs/ containing the original app.log file.  
     b) A checksum ledger called  
        /home/user/backups/backup_checksums.log  
        to which you must append ONE line whose exact format is:  
            2025-01-01,logs_backup_2025-01-01.tar.gz,SHA256_HASH  
        – “SHA256_HASH” must be the real 64-character hexadecimal hash of the
        archive created in step 2a.  
        – No leading or trailing spaces; end the line with a single newline.  
        – Do not add any extra lines or headers to the file.  
     c) An alert-flag file located at  
        /home/user/monitoring/alerts.conf  
        containing exactly one line:  
            BackupSuccess: true  
        (case-sensitive, no extra spaces, no newline after the line).

  3. Verification rules (what the automated tests will check):  
     • /home/user/backups/logs_backup_2025-01-01.tar.gz exists, is a valid
       gzip-compressed tar archive, and contains a path logs/app.log.  
     • /home/user/backups/backup_checksums.log exists, contains exactly one
       line in the comma-separated format described above, and the SHA-256
       value on that line matches the checksum of the archive.  
     • /home/user/monitoring/alerts.conf exists and contains the exact text
       “BackupSuccess: true”.  
     • The original source directory /home/user/app/logs and its app.log
       file remain unchanged.

  4. Constraints  
     • Work only inside /home/user; do not use sudo.  
     • You may assume that /home/user/backups and
       /home/user/monitoring do NOT yet exist; create them if needed.  
     • Keep the number of shell commands you use small (roughly 3–4), just
       as the monitoring specialist plans to embed them in a cronjob later.

  Produce the required files; no additional output is necessary.
truth:
<!-- Pre-existing data available to the agent when the scenario starts -->
  <initial_state>
    <directory path="/home/user/app/logs"/>
    <file path="/home/user/app/logs/app.log">
2024-01-05 12:00:00 Application started
2024-01-05 12:05:00 Application running
    </file>
  </initial_state>

  <!-- Expected end-state (values in angle brackets are placeholders the test
       suite will compute at runtime) -->
  <expected_state>
    <file path="/home/user/backups/logs_backup_2025-01-01.tar.gz">
      <contains path_inside_archive="logs/app.log"/>
    </file>

    <file path="/home/user/backups/backup_checksums.log">
      <line format="2025-01-01,logs_backup_2025-01-01.tar.gz,[0-9a-f]{64}"/>
      <checksum_match file="/home/user/backups/logs_backup_2025-01-01.tar.gz"/>
    </file>

    <file path="/home/user/monitoring/alerts.conf">
BackupSuccess: true
    </file>
  </expected_state>
name: task_000000_b9fe9eec
